spec_match.rationale: Combines counter() and attr() within the content property's alt text slot inside ::scroll-marker to demonstrate a highly advanced, real-world accessibility pattern.

interactive.rationale: The carousel is scrollable, but the accessibility readout is static and does not update to reflect the currently scrolled slide's name, and the scroll-markers themselves require experimental flags.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Frames the carousel scroll-marker use case perfectly as an ideal candidate for counter-based accessibility labels.

edge_cases.rationale: Lacks a fallback for browsers that do not support the highly experimental ::scroll-marker pseudo-element, making the dots invisible.

references.rationale: Properly references the CSS Content spec and ChromeStatus.

design_system.rationale: Integrates smoothly with standard design variables and custom fonts.

openQuestions.title: Make scroll-marker a11y readout update dynamically on scroll
openQuestions.detail: The accessibility readout box currently has a hardcoded string for Chapter 1. Since the carousel is scrollable, the readout should update dynamically using an intersection observer or scroll listener to show the accessible name of the active slide's scroll-marker.

openQuestions.title: Add a visual fallback for scroll-markers when ::scroll-marker is unsupported
openQuestions.detail: ::scroll-marker-group and ::scroll-marker are highly experimental CSS features. When they are not supported, the navigation dots do not render at all. Adding a lightweight JS fallback to render simulated navigation dots allows the user to see and interact with the demo on all browsers.

summary: A very innovative demo pairing scroll markers with custom accessibility alt text. However, the lack of a visual fallback for unsupported browsers and the static nature of the accessibility readout make the interactive experience somewhat limited.
